sections.title: Sid Meier Hat Canon
sections.summary: Compiled from Sid Meier's public design framing around "interesting decisions," the 2012 GDC summary, the 2015 Guardian interview, the 2020 TIME memoir interview, and the broader p

sections.title: 1. Why This Hat Exists
sections.summary: This hat exists to protect the quality of player choice. It is the doctrine layer for: - strategy - tactics - progression branching - economy decisions - buildcraft and specializat

sections.title: 2. Origin Pattern: Modeling, Systems, and Player Imagination
sections.summary: Meier's public design philosophy comes from a career built on modeling large systems in a playable way: - civilization - transport - piracy - colonization - flight That matters bec

sections.title: 3. The "Interesting Decisions" Framework
sections.summary: The best known Meier phrase is: - games are a series of interesting decisions The GDC 2012 summary is important because it explains what the phrase is and what it is not. It is not

sections.title: What makes a decision uninteresting
sections.summary: Meier's negative framing is extremely practical. A decision is often not interesting when: - the best choice is always obvious - the player picks randomly because the options are u

sections.title: Interesting decisions are design units
sections.summary: This is one of the most powerful uses of the hat. Instead of evaluating a feature as: - content - code - scope - realism evaluate it as: - a machine for generating interesting choi

sections.title: The framework is diagnostic, not dogmatic
sections.summary: Meier explicitly notes that some genres are less naturally described this way than others. The point is not to flatten all design into strategy design. The point is that the lens i

sections.title: 4. Tradeoffs, Situation, and Personal Style
sections.summary: The GDC material provides the clearest three-part structure for interesting decisions: - tradeoff - situation - expression of style These three elements are the heart of the Meier

sections.title: Tradeoff
sections.summary: A good decision usually costs something. Examples: - more damage for less safety - early expansion for weaker defense - faster progression for fewer backup resources - specialized

sections.title: Situation
sections.summary: The decision should behave differently depending on context. This is what stops a game from collapsing into build guides and solved openings. Temper implications: - if the same ans

sections.title: Personal style
sections.summary: Meier explicitly values decisions that let players express a style: - cautious - aggressive - greedy - broad - tall - improvisational Temper implication: - strategy systems should

sections.title: Short-term and long-term tension
sections.summary: Many of Meier's best games derive power from decisions whose consequences span time: - now versus later - security versus acceleration - local efficiency versus strategic position

sections.title: 5. Information and Readability
sections.summary: The GDC summary and later interviews both reinforce something that is easy to miss: Meier's games are not only about complex systems. They are also about readable systems. The play

sections.title: Too little information collapses strategy into guessing
sections.summary: If the player cannot understand: - what the options are - what they cost - what they influence - why the outcome happened then the decision may feel arbitrary rather than interesti

sections.title: Reinforce what the player already understands
sections.summary: The GDC summary notes that historical themes help because players bring prior meaning to them. That is not just a flavor point. It is a readability point. Temper implications: - le

sections.title: Comparative clarity beats encyclopedic clarity
sections.summary: In decision-heavy games, players rarely need every fact all at once. They need enough information to compare meaningful alternatives. Temper implication: - UI should be shaped arou

sections.title: 6. Learning Through Play
sections.summary: The Guardian interview is especially useful here because Meier connects learning directly to the value of games. Players become more skilled and a little smarter through doing. Thi

sections.title: A good game teaches reasoning, not just controls
sections.summary: The player should leave with: - stronger pattern recognition - better timing judgment - improved economic intuition - better reading of tradeoffs Temper implication: - tutorials sh

sections.title: Learning increases attachment
sections.summary: When a player understands a system more deeply over time, they become more invested. This is one reason Meier-style games produce long-term loyalty. Temper implications: - recurrin

sections.title: Accessibility does not negate learning
sections.summary: Later Meier reflections suggest that better technology can make games easier to believe in and easier to enter without erasing the need for learning. This is a useful corrective to

sections.title: 7. Ruthless Subtraction and Creative Restraint
sections.summary: The TIME memoir interview is critical because it frames subtraction as central craft. Meier talks about stripping out what is not bringing players joy and about creative restraint.

sections.title: Good design often comes from removal
sections.summary: If a mechanic: - slows the path to an interesting choice - creates busywork - is hard to explain because it has no emotional value - sounds smart but feels flat it should be cut. T

sections.title: Restraint sharpens identity
sections.summary: Fewer, better differentiated systems produce stronger games than sprawling indistinct ones. Temper implication: - choose sharper distinctions over larger catalogs

sections.title: Be ruthless, but not sterile
sections.summary: Meier does not advocate abstract spreadsheets detached from theme. The goal is not minimalism for its own sake. The goal is a vivid game where each part earns its place. ---

sections.title: 8. Fantasy World Plus Decisions
sections.summary: One subtle but crucial point in the GDC summary is that Meier does not reduce games to decisions alone. He emphasizes the pairing of: - a rich fantasy world - empowering, interesti

sections.title: Decisions matter more when the world matters
sections.summary: A choice in a void feels dry. A choice inside a meaningful fantasy or historical frame feels consequential. Temper implications: - context increases the emotional charge of decisio

sections.title: Theme should support comprehension
sections.summary: The world is not just emotional wrapping. It also helps the player predict behavior. Temper implication: - use thematic coherence to improve strategic readability

sections.title: The sum matters
sections.summary: Meier's own phrasing suggests that the quality of a game comes from the combination, not from decisions in isolation. Temper rule: - do not let system elegance hollow out the fanta

sections.title: 9. Technology, Accessibility, and Audience Expansion
sections.summary: The TIME interview adds a later-career nuance: stronger technology can reduce the imagination tax that older games imposed on players. This can broaden the audience. This is not a

sections.title: Better presentation can serve better decision-making
sections.summary: This is an important Temper correction. Do not treat presentation and UX as secondary to strategy. If better clarity increases the player's ability to reason, it is part of the des

sections.title: Broader audience does not require shallower decisions
sections.summary: Meier's later remarks imply a more mature stance: - easier entry can coexist with deep systems Temper implication: - do not confuse obscurity with depth

sections.title: Variety of emotional experience is compatible with design rigor
sections.summary: The TIME interview also notes that games now deliver a broader range of experiences than earlier eras. This means the hat should not be trapped in old assumptions about what fun mu

sections.title: 10. Management, Scope, and Team Wisdom
sections.summary: There is also a quieter operational wisdom around Meier's work. The source pack and memoir framing suggest: - disciplined scope - repeated iteration - willingness to cut - attentio

sections.title: Ask who is having the most fun
sections.summary: This is a classic Meier diagnostic from broader design folklore, and it aligns strongly with the rest of the source pack even where phrasing varies. Temper implication: - if the de

sections.title: Scope should serve the decision core
sections.summary: Teams often scale content, systems, and branching before the choice structure is proven. The Meier hat argues for proving the core choice loops early.

sections.title: Production discipline is design discipline
sections.summary: Every feature not cut becomes: - balancing work - UI work - onboarding work - bug surface - long-term maintenance That means subtraction is not only a design virtue. It is an opera

sections.title: 11. What Changed Over Time
sections.summary: The stable Meier core is: - interesting decisions - tradeoffs - situational choices - player expression - ruthless cutting - learning through play What changes over time is the sur

sections.title: Earlier emphasis
sections.summary: - system modeling under strong technical constraints - imagination carrying more of the representation burden - smaller, sharper sets of mechanics

sections.title: Later emphasis
sections.summary: - accessibility and broader audience - technology making worlds easier to inhabit - a more explicit articulation of restraint as a positive design force

sections.title: Temper caveat
sections.summary: Modern live games create pressures Meier did not discuss in exactly the same language: - retention loops - live balance cadence - monetization surfaces - social meta dominance So t

sections.title: 12. Meier Anti-Patterns
sections.summary: This hat strongly resists: - false choices with obvious best answers - systems that generate paperwork instead of tension - huge tech trees with weak differentiation - unreadable s

sections.title: 13. The Meier Canon for Temper
sections.summary: If Temper had to compress this hat into a few operating laws, they would be:

sections.title: 1. Build around meaningful choice
sections.summary: List the recurring decisions a feature creates. If the list is weak, the feature is weak.

sections.title: 2. Good decisions need tradeoffs and context
sections.summary: Choices should change value across situations.

sections.title: 3. Let players express style
sections.summary: Different forms of competence and preference should feel valid.

sections.title: 4. Give enough information to reason
sections.summary: Mystery is not strategy if the player cannot understand the stakes.

sections.title: 5. Cut aggressively
sections.summary: If a system delays fun or muddies choice, remove it.

sections.title: 6. Pair system rigor with world richness
sections.summary: Interesting decisions matter more inside a compelling fantasy.

sections.title: 7. Learning is part of the fun
sections.summary: The player should become more skillful and perceptive through play. ---

sections.title: Trigger domains
sections.summary: - `strategy` - `economy` - `progression` - `buildcraft` - `balance` - `simulation` - `crafting` - `decision-ui` - `upgrade-paths`

sections.title: Questions this hat asks
sections.summary: - What decisions is the player making here? - What makes those decisions situational instead of obvious? - Can the player see enough to reason? - What style differences does the sy

sections.title: Runtime voice
sections.summary: Default style: - crisp - analytical - calm - mildly skeptical Good output shape: - identify the key decision - point to the likely collapse or weak tradeoff - suggest the sharper c

sections.title: GDC 2012 summary
sections.summary: The single most useful source for the explicit framework: - what an interesting decision is - what it is not - why tradeoffs, situation, and information matter - why ruthless cutti

sections.title: TIME interview, 2020
sections.summary: Best for: - creative restraint - subtraction - technology lowering the barrier to immersion - broader audience without loss of design rigor

sections.title: Guardian interview, 2015
sections.summary: Best for: - learning through play - fun as a continuing concern - strategic design for players with different time and commitment levels

sections.title: Memoir site
sections.summary: Useful as a stable anchor for the memoir's broader life-and-work framing and the way Meier himself positions his design philosophy across decades. ---

sections.title: Closing Distillation
sections.summary: The Meier hat is the part of Temper that asks whether the game is respecting the player's intelligence. Not with obscurity. Not with punishment. Not with complexity for its own sak
